messages.content:
The workspace contains `perf.py` — a module whose functions are CORRECT but
too slow for production. Your job: make every function fast enough to pass
the timing budgets below while keeping the results identical.

Functions and their required behavior (unchanged):

- `common_elements(a, b)` — sorted list of values appearing in both lists
- `fib(n)` — nth Fibonacci number (fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1)
- `count_pairs(nums, target)` — number of index pairs i<j with
  nums[i]+nums[j] == target
- `dedupe_keep_order(items)` — remove duplicates, preserving first-seen order

Timing budgets (generous — any reasonable algorithmic fix sails through):
- `common_elements` on two 200,000-element lists: under 2 seconds
- `fib(300)`: under 2 seconds
- `count_pairs` on 100,000 elements: under 2 seconds
- `dedupe_keep_order` on 200,000 items: under 2 seconds

Rules: keep the function names and signatures; standard library only; the
module must still import cleanly. Correctness is tested with exact expected
values — optimize the algorithms, don't approximate.

text:
All four functions are optimized and verified — results match the originals exactly, and every budget is cleared by orders of magnitude.

Changes to `perf.py`:
- **`common_elements`**: `sorted(set(a) & set(b))` — set intersection replaces the O(n·m) membership scans. 0.028s on two 200k lists (budget 2s).
- **`fib`**: iterative accumulation replaces exponential recursion; kept the `n < 2` guard so small/negative inputs behave identically. fib(300) is instant and returns the exact 63-digit value.
- **`count_pairs`**: single pass with a counts dict — for each element, add how many earlier elements complete the target sum. O(n); 0.006s on 100k elements.
- **`dedupe_keep_order`**: a `seen` set alongside the output list, replacing the O(n²) list scan. 0.003s on 200k items.

Verification: I fuzzed each function against the original slow implementation on 200 random small inputs (exact-equality asserts, all passed), checked known Fibonacci values, then timed at the budget sizes above. The scratch test script was removed afterward, leaving only the updated `perf.py`.
